transcript:
Right now you're watching pale, tadpole-shaped trails swim across black — bright heads dragging fading blue tails, a few free-floating rings drifting nearby. It looks organic, almost microscopic, like something alive under a slide.

Here's the part that got me: each trail isn't drawing where it's going — it's reacting to what's already there. Look at the "findNearbySegments" function: every growth tip scans a spatial grid for marks placed earlier, and in "update," it bends its angle toward the average position of those neighbors — "curve toward the community forming in my absence," the comment says. The tip has no memory of the whole picture, just local neighbors it stumbles into.

And notice "drawSegment" — brightness and hue shift with age, so what you're seeing isn't just position, it's time made visible: bright cyan is newborn, deep blue-violet is memory fading toward "fadedMemory."

So the braiding you see isn't planned by anything — it's thousands of blind, local reactions accumulating into something that only looks intentional from where you're standing.
